title: Mission 05 — High Density Altitude
summary: A hot-day performance mission focused on density altitude, climb performance, loading tradeoffs, and disciplined abort/diversion decisions. This mission punishes magical thinking about the turbo T182T.

weather.summary: Very hot afternoon conditions are driving density altitude and reducing takeoff, climb, and go-around margins even though weather remains VFR.

notams: KLEX intersection departure requests suspended due performance concerns in heat of day.
KLOU density altitude advisory in effect during afternoon operations.

personalMinimums: No unnecessary weight on hot-day departures
Use full runway and abort points on high DA days
If climb performance is marginal, stop and fix the plan
Turbocharging helps power, not wing performance

events.title: Performance Mindset
events.prompt: What is the most accurate statement about this hot-day mission in a turbo T182T?

options.label: The turbo helps maintain power, but density altitude still hurts climb, acceleration, and go-around performance.

consequence.feedback: Correct. Turbocharging does not remove the aerodynamic penalties of thin air.
consequence.commMessage: Hot-day performance brief complete: turbo helps power, not all performance.

options.label: The turbo means density altitude is basically not a concern for this mission.

consequence.feedback: Incorrect. That misconception is exactly what gets pilots trapped on hot-day operations.

options.label: Density altitude mostly matters only on landing, not takeoff or climb.

consequence.feedback: Incorrect. The biggest penalty often appears in takeoff and climb performance.

events.title: Weight Tradeoff
events.prompt: What loading choice best fits a hot-day density altitude departure?

options.label: Launch with the planned 52 gallons and minimal baggage to preserve performance while keeping strong reserve.

consequence.feedback: Correct. The best answer preserves both reserve and climb margin.

options.label: Top off because more fuel is always the safest answer regardless of climb performance.

consequence.feedback: Weak. Excess fuel can erase the very performance margin you need on a high-DA day.

options.label: Take very little fuel to maximize climb, even if reserve becomes thin.

consequence.feedback: Incorrect. You solve one risk by creating another.

events.title: Takeoff Briefing
events.prompt: Which departure brief best fits this mission?

options.label: Use full runway, identify an abort point, and reject the takeoff if acceleration or engine indications are not right.

consequence.feedback: Correct. Hot-day departures demand a disciplined abort mindset.
consequence.commMessage: Takeoff brief complete: full runway, acceleration check, abort point identified.

options.label: Use an intersection to save taxi time because the airplane is powerful enough.

consequence.feedback: Incorrect. Giving away runway on a high-density-altitude departure is poor planning.

options.label: Skip the abort brief because if the engine is running, the takeoff will probably work out.

consequence.feedback: Weak ADM. The whole point of the brief is to decide before the pressure spikes.

events.title: Poor Initial Climb
events.prompt: After takeoff you are climbing, but much more slowly than expected in the heat. What is the best response?

options.label: Pitch for the appropriate climb profile, reduce drag, and be ready to level off or return if performance remains inadequate.

consequence.feedback: Correct. Protect airspeed first, then decide whether continuing is still justified.
consequence.commMessage: Climb is weaker than expected; prioritizing airspeed and reassessing the plan.

options.label: Raise the nose further to force a stronger climb.

consequence.feedback: Incorrect. Pulling harder in weak climb conditions risks getting slow and making things worse.

options.label: Continue because the airplane is climbing, even if the margin is poor.

consequence.feedback: Weak. Marginal climb performance should trigger a real reassessment, not passive hope.

events.title: Go-Around Margin Discussion
events.prompt: ATC mentions gusts near Louisville and asks if you can accept a short approach. What is the best answer?

options.label: Decline the short approach and request spacing so you keep a stable hot-day arrival and go-around margin.

consequence.feedback: Correct. Stable spacing matters more than convenience when performance margins are thinner.
consequence.commMessage: Unable short approach due density-altitude performance concerns; request normal spacing.

options.label: Accept the short approach to show confidence and keep traffic moving.

consequence.feedback: Poor trade. Confidence is not the same thing as respecting reduced go-around margin.

options.label: Say nothing and try to make it work.

consequence.feedback: Incorrect. If you need spacing, communicate it clearly.

events.title: High-DA Arrival Discipline
events.prompt: On final you are fast and slightly high. What is the best hot-day performance decision?

options.label: Go around early or divert if needed instead of trying to rescue a poor hot-day approach.

consequence.feedback: Correct. High density altitude reduces your landing and go-around margin, so unstable means unstable.
consequence.commMessage: Tower, Skylane 734JP going around due unstable approach.

options.label: Force the landing because the runway is long and you are already close.

consequence.feedback: Incorrect. Long runway is not permission to force an unstable approach in degraded performance conditions.

options.label: Use aggressive late corrections and salvage it at the threshold.

consequence.feedback: Weak. The need for aggressive late corrections is the warning sign, not the plan.
